title: Classroom Activities for Student AI Literacy
subtitle: Ready-to-run lesson outlines for teachers developing student AI literacy in subject classes

alsoUsefulFor: academic_coordinator (when coordinating AI literacy across departments)
cambridge_coordinator (when aligning to Cambridge Digital Literacy)

_meta.purpose: When you decide to teach students about AI in your subject class, this file gives you ready-to-run lesson outlines. Not a separate AI curriculum — small activities you slot into your existing teaching once or twice per term.
_meta.writingNote: Plain English. Concrete activities. Each lesson outline fits on one screen of reading. Adapt to your class.
alignsWith: Eduversal v1.0 Part 2 (Student AI Competency Framework): age-appropriate learning outcomes across 4 strands (Understanding AI / Using AI Responsibly / Evaluating AI Outputs / AI and Society)
Cambridge International Feb 2026 Primary + Lower Secondary Digital Literacy curriculum refresh
Permendikdasmen 13/2025 (Koding & AI elective context — these activities are SUBJECT-INTEGRATED, not the standalone elective)

howToUseThisFile.frequency: Aim for ONE activity per term per class. Three per year is enough to develop student AI literacy without taking time away from subject learning. More is fine but not required.
howToUseThisFile.subjectIntegration: These are SUBJECT lessons that develop AI literacy as a side effect, not separate 'AI lessons'. A Year 8 English teacher critiquing an AI-generated essay is teaching English AND teaching AI literacy at the same time. That's the design.
howToUseThisFile.preparation: Most activities need 20-30 minutes of teacher preparation (generating an AI output, adapting a worksheet). Read the activity, prepare in advance. Don't try to improvise these in front of students.
howToUseThisFile.ageBandSelection: Choose the age band that matches your students. Primary activities work for Years 1-6. Lower Secondary for Years 7-9. Upper Secondary for Years 10-12. Don't run an Upper Secondary activity with Year 7s — the cognitive demand is different.
howToUseThisFile.afterEachActivity: Note what worked. Tell your Subject Leader. Patterns spotted across the department go to your Academic Coordinator.

primaryActivities._overview: At Primary level, students develop AWARENESS of AI as a kind of technology. Direct hands-on AI use is limited and teacher-led. Focus is on conceptual understanding: what AI is, what it can and cannot do, why humans are always in charge.

activities.title: AI in My Day
activities.teacherPrep: 10 minutes — prepare a worksheet (template in resources)
activities.learningObjective: Students can identify examples of AI in their everyday life and explain in simple terms what makes them AI.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Show 3 photos — a voice assistant, a video recommendation screen, a smart home device. Ask 'What do all of these have in common?' Collect ideas.

Main (20 min): Introduce 'artificial intelligence' as 'computer programs that learn from examples to do tasks that seem clever.' Students complete a worksheet listing 5 AI examples they've met (at home, at school, online) and what each one does.
Discussion (10 min): Class shares examples. Teacher draws out: AI follows patterns it has learned. AI doesn't understand like humans do. People made AI; people choose how to use it.
Exit ticket (3 min): One sentence — 'AI is good at ____, but not as good as humans at ____.'
materials: Three photos (voice assistant, recommendation feed, smart home)
Worksheet (5 examples × 2 columns: what is it / what does it do)
activities.extension: For students who finish early: draw a picture of an AI tool helping someone — and a picture of something AI cannot do.
activities.assessmentIdea: Collect exit tickets. Look for: did the student name a real AI example? Did they identify something AI cannot do? This is a quick formative check, not a grade.
activities.redLines: Teacher uses AI tools to PREPARE this lesson if needed. Students do NOT directly use AI tools in this activity — exposure only.

activities.title: AI Makes Mistakes Too
activities.teacherPrep: 20 minutes — generate an AI response with one obvious error embedded (your prompt: 'Write a paragraph for Year 5 students about [topic], and include one small mistake that I will get them to find')
activities.learningObjective: Students understand that AI can make mistakes even when it sounds confident, and they know to check AI information against other sources.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Show the AI-generated paragraph on screen. Read it together. Ask 'Does anyone notice anything that doesn't sound right?'

Main (25 min): In pairs, students hunt for the mistake. They check against a textbook or a trusted source you've provided. When pairs find the mistake, they describe what it is and how they confirmed it was wrong.

Discussion (10 min): Why might AI make this kind of mistake? (AI learns from many examples; sometimes the examples are wrong; AI doesn't really know if something is true.) What should we always do with AI answers? (Check them.)
materials: AI-generated paragraph with one factual error
Trusted source for checking (textbook page, school library book, teacher-prepared fact sheet)
activities.extension: Students write their own 'AI mistake' — a sentence that sounds confident but is wrong. They swap with a partner who has to spot it.
activities.assessmentIdea: Listen during pair work. Did students use the trusted source to confirm? Did they describe the mistake clearly?
activities.redLines: Teacher generates the AI output BEFORE the lesson. Students do not query AI directly in this activity.

activities.title: AI or Human?
activities.teacherPrep: 15 minutes — prepare 6 short texts: 3 written by humans (children's work or short paragraphs you've written), 3 generated by AI. Label them A-F.
activities.learningObjective: Students start to notice the patterns and limitations of AI-generated text by comparing it with human writing.
lessonOutline: Starter (3 min): Tell students: 'Some of these were written by people. Some were written by AI. Your job is to guess which is which.'
Main (15 min): Students work individually first, marking each piece H (human) or AI. Then they discuss in pairs and revise. Collect predictions.

Reveal + discussion (12 min): Reveal which were AI. Discuss: what clues did students use? Was AI text harder or easier to spot than expected? Common observations: AI text is often grammatically perfect but feels 'flat'; human text often has more specific personal detail or surprises.
materials: Recording sheet (which is H, which is AI, why?)
activities.extension: Take one of the AI texts. Students rewrite it to sound more human — adding a specific detail, a question, a surprising opinion.
activities.assessmentIdea: Track which clues students cite. Students who notice STRUCTURAL patterns ('it always uses three examples') are deeper than students who guess randomly.
activities.redLines: Activity is about RECOGNITION, not about training students to be AI detectors. Detection at this level is unreliable — keep the focus on understanding patterns, not on cheating accusations.

lowerSecondaryActivities._overview: At Lower Secondary, students start guided use of AI tools with explicit teacher instruction. The activities below balance hands-on use (with school-approved safe tools) and critical evaluation. Cambridge International's Feb 2026 Digital Literacy refresh covers this age band specifically — these activities support that.
lowerSecondaryActivities._toolsForStudentUse: If students use AI live in these activities, use only SchoolAI / Khanmigo / Curipod (teacher-monitored). Never general-public ChatGPT / Claude / Gemini for direct student access. Manifest ref: toolRecommendations.categories.studentFacingSafeAi

activities.title: Critique an AI Essay
activities.teacherPrep: 25 minutes — generate an AI essay on a topic your students have studied. Prompt the AI for a response that's plausible but contains 3 specific weaknesses: one factual or logical error, one cultural-bias issue (Western-default examples in an Indonesian context), and one missing nuance (oversimplification of a contested issue). Print copies. Prepare an answer key for yourself.
activities.learningObjective: Students can identify specific weaknesses in AI-generated essays and understand why AI produces these kinds of errors.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Display the essay. Read the first paragraph together. Ask 'Does this look like good writing on first impression?' Most students will say yes.

Individual annotation (15 min): Students mark on the printed essay: anything that looks wrong, anything that feels superficial, anything missing. They use a four-colour code: factual issues / bias issues / oversimplification / 'sounds AI'.
Pair discussion (10 min): Students compare annotations. They agree on the top 3 issues to share with the class.

Whole-class consolidation (15 min): Collect issues on the board. Teacher reveals the 3 specific weaknesses you embedded. Discuss: why does AI produce these kinds of errors? (Trained on existing text — inherits the biases of that text; doesn't know what's true; doesn't know your specific Indonesian context.)
Exit reflection (5 min): One sentence — 'When I use AI to help me learn, I will always ____.'
materials: Printed copies of the AI essay (one per student)
Teacher answer key (for your eyes only — do NOT share before the discussion)
activities.extension: Students rewrite one paragraph of the AI essay, fixing the issues they identified.
activities.assessmentIdea: Check exit reflections. Are students articulating a specific habit ('I will check facts against two sources', 'I will look for Indonesian examples') rather than a vague intention ('I will be careful')?
activities.redLines: Teacher generates the essay BEFORE the lesson, not live with students watching. Don't let this lesson drift into 'how to write better AI prompts' — the goal is critical evaluation, not prompt optimisation.

activities.title: Supervised Research with AI (using SchoolAI)
activities.teacherPrep: 20 minutes — set up a SchoolAI session (or your school's approved teacher-monitored AI). Prepare 4-5 research questions appropriate for the topic. Pre-review them yourself.
activities.learningObjective: Students use AI as a research starting point under teacher supervision, then verify findings against trusted sources. They learn that AI is a starting point, not an ending point.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Show how to ask a research question well. Bad: 'Tell me about X.' Better: 'Explain X in 3 paragraphs, for a Year 8 student, and list 3 follow-up questions I should ask.' Discuss why the second works better.

Phase 1 — AI starting point (15 min): Students work individually. Each uses SchoolAI (teacher dashboard open, you watch live) to ask one research question. They write a 4-sentence summary of what AI told them.

Phase 2 — verification (25 min): Students leave SchoolAI. Using textbooks, trusted websites, or your library, they verify 3 specific claims from the AI summary. Mark each claim ✓ (verified), ✗ (wrong), or ? (couldn't find).
Phase 3 — class share (10 min): Each student names one thing AI got right and one thing AI got wrong or couldn't be verified. Discuss patterns.
Exit reflection (5 min): 'AI was useful in my research because ____. AI was unreliable in my research because ____.'
materials: School-approved AI tool with teacher dashboard (SchoolAI or equivalent)
Trusted source materials (textbook, library, curated website list)
activities.extension: Students write a one-paragraph guide for next year's Year 8 students: 'How to use AI for research without getting it wrong.'
activities.assessmentIdea: Check the verification phase. Did students actually use trusted sources, or did they just transcribe AI answers? The verification act is the assessable skill.
activities.redLines: Use ONLY a teacher-monitored AI tool. Never let students open ChatGPT / Claude / Gemini directly during this activity. Don't let students paste personal information into AI even with teacher monitoring.

activities.title: AI and Fairness
activities.teacherPrep: 15 minutes — gather 2-3 short news stories or examples of AI bias (job-application AI rejecting certain demographics; facial recognition failing for darker skin tones; AI translation defaulting to gender stereotypes). Print for students.
activities.learningObjective: Students understand that AI systems can perpetuate or amplify bias, and they can discuss issues of fairness in technology.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Ask 'Can a computer be unfair?' Collect initial views — most will say no, computers just follow rules.
Reading (15 min): In groups, students read one news story / example each. They note: what happened, who was disadvantaged, why did AI do this?

Group share (15 min): Each group presents 'their' example to the class. Teacher draws out the common pattern: AI learned from past data; the past data had unfair patterns; AI repeated those patterns at scale.
Discussion (10 min): What should be done? Who is responsible — the AI, the people who made it, the people who use it? Students argue different positions.
Exit reflection (5 min): 'One way to make AI fairer is ____.'
activities.extension: Students design a school rule for fair AI use — what should our school do to make sure AI is used fairly?
activities.assessmentIdea: Listen to the group discussions. Are students identifying SOURCE of bias (training data, design choices) or just describing symptoms? Source-thinking is the higher skill.
activities.redLines: Use real, current examples — not made-up scenarios. AI bias is real; trivialising it teaches the wrong lesson. Don't let the discussion become 'AI is evil' — the point is human choices shape AI outcomes.

activities.title: Disclose Your AI Use
activities.teacherPrep: 10 minutes — prepare 3 example student work samples with AI use described differently (one honestly disclosing 'I used AI to draft my opening paragraph then rewrote it'; one vaguely 'AI helped'; one undisclosed AI use you can detect).
activities.learningObjective: Students adopt the habit of disclosing AI use in their work and understand why disclosure matters.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Read the school's AI use policy aloud — focus on the disclosure expectation. Ask why this rule exists.

Worked examples (15 min): Show the 3 sample disclosures. Students rate each: honest / vague / missing. Discuss what a good disclosure looks like (specific about what AI did + what you did + what you learned).

Adoption (10 min): Introduce the class norm: 'From this term, every piece of writing you submit ends with one sentence describing if and how you used AI.' Practise writing one disclosure for a recent piece of work.
Ongoing: For the rest of the term, every submission includes a disclosure line. Light-touch enforcement — focus on building the habit, not on catching out students.
activities.extension: Students draft a one-paragraph guide for their parents explaining the school's disclosure norm.
activities.assessmentIdea: Track disclosure quality across the term. Are disclosures getting more specific over time? That's the growth.
activities.redLines: Disclosure is NOT a confession — it's a normalised habit. Don't treat disclosed AI use as evidence of misconduct unless the policy was specifically broken. Build trust, not surveillance.
activities.provenance: synthesis (reference:eduversal-v1-part2-student.lower_secondary.strand2; Cambridge Exams Officers Guide Phase 3 framing)

upperSecondaryActivities._overview: At Upper Secondary, students develop sophisticated AI literacy preparing them for higher education and AI-integrated work. Activities support independent AI use with strong critical evaluation, academic integrity navigation, and engagement with AI's social implications.
upperSecondaryActivities._cambridgeNote: For Cambridge IGCSE and AS/A Level coursework, defer to Cambridge's Exams Officers Guide Phase 3 on AI use. These activities support learning ABOUT AI; they do not change Cambridge's rules on AI USE in coursework.

activities.title: Prompt Engineering Workshop
activities.teacherPrep: 30 minutes — prepare 4 sample prompts (one one-line query, one moderate context, one detailed 4-part mega-prompt, one with explicit constraints). Generate output for each so students see the difference. Print outputs.
activities.learningObjective: Students learn the 4-part mega-prompt structure (Role + Context + Task + Constraints) and apply it to subject tasks, understanding that iterative prompting beats one-shot prompting.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Show all 4 outputs side by side. 'These come from 4 different prompts. Which output looks most useful? Why?'
Direct instruction (15 min): Reveal the 4 prompts. Walk through the structure. Discuss what each part adds. Students take notes.
Practice (25 min): Each student writes a 4-part prompt for one subject task they currently have. They run it through the school-approved AI tool. They iterate once with a follow-up prompt.
Share + reflect (15 min): Pairs share their best prompt and the output. Discuss patterns: what kinds of constraints worked? What follow-up prompts produced the biggest jump in quality?
materials: Access to school-approved AI tool (teacher-monitored if your school requires)
activities.extension: Students build a personal prompt library — 3-5 prompts they will reuse for common task types in their subjects.
activities.assessmentIdea: Review students' prompts: do they include role, context, task, AND constraints? Did the follow-up turn meaningfully improve the output?
activities.redLines: Students use only school-approved AI tools. Do not let students use this lesson as a chance to ask AI to do their actual coursework — frame it as 'learning the skill', not 'doing the assignment'.

activities.title: When AI Use Is Appropriate (Cambridge-aligned)
activities.teacherPrep: 20 minutes — gather Cambridge's coursework AI guidance (Exams Officers Guide Phase 3). Prepare 5 scenarios: AI to brainstorm ideas / AI to draft a thesis statement / AI to outline an essay / AI to write a paragraph / AI to write the whole piece. Print the scenarios.
activities.learningObjective: Students exercise sophisticated judgement about when AI use is appropriate, beneficial, or inappropriate — specifically for Cambridge coursework contexts.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Ask 'Is it OK to use AI for your IGCSE coursework?' Collect varied views.
Cambridge guidance reading (10 min): Read the relevant excerpt from Cambridge's coursework AI policy. Students take notes on what is and isn't allowed.

Scenario discussion (25 min): In groups, students rank the 5 scenarios from 'clearly OK' to 'clearly not OK', referring to Cambridge's guidance. They have to JUSTIFY each ranking with reference to the policy.
Whole-class consolidation (10 min): Compare rankings across groups. Where do groups disagree? Teacher provides the school's interpretation. Settle on the class consensus aligned to Cambridge's rules.
materials: Cambridge coursework AI guidance excerpt (current version)
activities.extension: Students write a one-paragraph 'decision rule' they will use when starting any coursework piece: 'Before using AI for this piece, I will check ____.'
activities.assessmentIdea: Listen to group justifications. Are students citing the policy, or just expressing personal opinions? Citation discipline is the assessable skill.
activities.redLines: The Cambridge guidance is the authority. Don't let the lesson drift into 'what would be a good rule' — the rule already exists. Teach students to NAVIGATE the rule, not to invent their own.

activities.title: AI Critique at Research Level
activities.teacherPrep: 30 minutes — prepare an AI-generated mini-research response (~500 words) on a topic with contested scholarly debate. Embed: one fabricated citation (made-up author + year), one oversimplification of a complex debate, one Western-default framing where Indonesian or Southeast Asian perspectives would matter.
activities.learningObjective: Students apply rigorous evaluation standards to AI outputs at research level, identifying subtle errors and biases that require careful analysis.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Read the AI response together. Ask 'On first read, does this look like decent research writing?' Most will say yes.

Three-pass evaluation (40 min). Students work individually then in pairs. Pass 1: verify every citation against actual sources (one fabricated citation will surface). Pass 2: identify any oversimplification of debate (multiple sources should be presented). Pass 3: identify default cultural framings (the Western-default issue).
Group synthesis (20 min): Pairs compare findings. Class develops a checklist: 'When evaluating AI research output, always check for ____.'
Personal commitment (10 min): Each student writes one sentence describing a research habit they will adopt because of this lesson.
materials: Access to library, databases, or trusted online sources for verification
activities.extension: Students rewrite the AI response, fixing the issues they identified and adding the missing perspectives.
activities.assessmentIdea: Did students FIND the fabricated citation? Citation verification is a key Upper Secondary skill — this lesson assesses it directly.
activities.redLines: Don't show the answer key until after the work. The point is for students to develop the habit, not to confirm what the teacher already told them.

activities.title: AI in My Future Field
activities.teacherPrep: 10 minutes — prepare a research question template
activities.learningObjective: Students discuss how AI is changing the field they intend to study or work in, and identify the skills they need to thrive alongside AI.
lessonOutline: Starter (5 min): Ask 'In 10 years, what will be different in your future career because of AI?' Collect initial thoughts.

Individual research (25 min): Each student researches AI's current impact on their intended field (medicine, law, engineering, art, teaching, etc.). They use AI itself as one source AND at least two trusted human sources. They note: what is AI doing in this field now? What is it likely to do in 5-10 years? What human skills will become MORE important?
Small-group share (15 min): Groups of 3-4 different fields share. They look for patterns: skills that seem to matter across all fields.

Whole-class consolidation (5 min): Teacher draws out: across all fields, the human skills that matter more in an AI-rich world tend to be judgement, ethics, communication, creativity, empathy. AI accelerates the doing; humans still decide what's worth doing.
Exit reflection: 'One skill I will develop in school to prepare for AI in my field is ____.'
materials: Research template (3-column: now / 5-10 years / human skills needed)
Access to varied sources (school library + AI tool + trusted websites)
activities.extension: Students write a one-page reflection for their portfolio: 'Why I'm preparing for AI in my future career — and how I'm doing it.'
activities.assessmentIdea: Look at the human-skills list. Are students moving past surface answers ('be creative') to specific, actionable skills ('learn to evaluate AI claims', 'develop ethical judgement')?
activities.redLines: Don't let this become a 'will AI take your job' panic conversation. Frame it as 'how do humans and AI work together' — that's the more realistic and more empowering frame.

subjectSpecificNotes._purpose: Per-subject calibration for the activities above. The activities work across subjects; these notes refine them per discipline.
mathematics: Critique-an-AI-output works well in maths with worked solutions — AI often gives plausibly-structured solutions with subtle errors. Students learn to verify each step.
Avoid using AI for novel proof strategies — AI is weak here, and the lesson would just demonstrate AI failure rather than build student skill.
Strong for misconception-targeting: AI can generate problems where the wrong answer surfaces a specific misconception.
english: AI essay critique is your most powerful activity — every English class above Year 8 should do this once per term.
AI tends to write flat prose with predictable structures. Students get good at spotting it within 2-3 exposures.
Use for vocabulary work: AI can generate definitions and example sentences, but its examples are often generic. Students learn to write better example sentences from real contexts.
sciences: AI is reasonable on physics/chemistry/biology factual content but weak on practical-skill assessment and real-world experimental design.
Strong activity: AI-generates a 'science explanation' with one factual error embedded. Students verify against the textbook.
For Cambridge IGCSE / AS-A Level practical papers, AI cannot replace lab experience — keep this clear in your discussion.
humanities: AI's biggest weakness in humanities is cultural-default framing — most AI outputs centre Western perspectives by default.

Strong activity: AI-generates a historical or geographical account. Students identify whose perspective is being centred and what's missing (Indonesian, Southeast Asian, indigenous, women's perspectives).
For contested topics (political history, current affairs), AI often presents a single 'balanced' view that flattens real debates. Students learn to ask: who disagrees with this and why?
bahasa_indonesia: Per the 2025-2026 Bahasa Indonesia LLM benchmark, Gemini 3.x and Claude Opus 4.6 currently produce the most natural Bahasa.
Strong activity: students compare AI-generated Bahasa text with native-Indonesian writing. Discuss register, idiom, cultural nuance.
AI's Bahasa often defaults to slightly-too-formal register for everyday contexts. Students learn that 'correct grammar' ≠ 'natural Bahasa'.
religion_pendidikan_agama: AI is fundamentally weak on theological nuance and Indonesian religious-context specifics. Activities here should focus on critical evaluation more than direct AI use.
Strong activity: AI explanation of a religious concept, students compare against authoritative sources (Quran, Bible, religious teacher's framing).
Avoid using AI to generate religious teaching content — the risk of error or unintended doctrinal framing is too high.

department_level: Subject Leaders should aim for ONE activity per term per class across the department. Three per year is enough.
Coordinate across departments: don't have every Year 8 subject doing AI critique the same week.
Track which activities worked. Note in your termly department report what you'd repeat and what you'd change.
school_level: Academic Coordinator should oversee distribution across year groups so students get exposure but no class is over-loaded.
Cambridge Coordinator should ensure activities targeting Year 10+ align with Cambridge's coursework guidance.
Reports to parents on student AI literacy should be specific ('your child can identify AI-generated bias') not vague ('your child has learned about AI').
network_level: Eduversal Academic Directorate may add new activities to this file based on what worked across schools. See contributionInvitation.
Activities here are starting points. School-specific adaptations are encouraged — preserve the learning objectives, change the materials.

pairedDocuments.promptLibrary: prompt-library.json — most activities reference specific prompts
pairedDocuments.redFlagsRedlines: redFlagsAndRedlines.json — every activity respects the red lines (especially rl_06 on personal accounts and the EAL false-positive warning on detection)
pairedDocuments.decisionTrees: decision-trees.json — referenced when student situations arise (suspected AI submission, parent questions)

contributionInvitation.purpose: The best activities come from teachers who ran them and learned what worked.
howToContribute: If you ran an activity from this file and adapted it, write up your adaptation (what you changed, why, what worked better).
If you developed your own activity from scratch and it worked across multiple classes, write it up using the template in this file (activityId / title / age band / etc.).
Submit to your Academic Coordinator → HQ Subject Specialist for review and possible inclusion in next year's revision.
whatGetsIncluded: Activities tested across at least 3 classes.
Activities with clear learning objectives traceable to Eduversal v1.0 Part 2 strands.
Activities that respect the red lines by design.

items: Network-approved student-facing AI tool — not yet confirmed for 2026-2027. Activities currently reference SchoolAI / Khanmigo / Curipod as category recommendations.
How these activities interact with Cambridge Digital Literacy curriculum refresh (Feb 2026) — close alignment is intended but Cambridge's own implementation guidance is still being finalised.
Whether Eduversal will adopt Permendikdasmen 13/2025 Koding & AI elective network-wide — if yes, these subject-integrated activities are a complement, not a replacement.
Religion / Pendidikan Agama is the most under-developed subject area in this file — religious specialists invited to contribute activities.

v1.0_2026-05-17.added: Initial release with 11 activities across three age bands (3 Primary + 4 Lower Secondary + 4 Upper Secondary).
v1.0_2026-05-17.strandsCovered: All four Eduversal v1.0 Part 2 strands represented at each age band.
knownAbsences: Religion / Pendidikan Agama activities — deferred pending religious-specialist contribution.
Arts subjects (music, visual art, drama) — generic activities adapt, but no arts-specific activities yet.
Specific Cambridge IGCSE / AS-A Level practical-paper alignment — Cambridge Coordinator review needed.
Bahasa Indonesia subject-specific activities (using Bahasa as the language of learning) — to be added by Bahasa specialists.

versionHistory.annualReviewExpected: Each Term 3, add new activities based on contributions. Retire activities that didn't work in practice.
